speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Nathan, you were just telling me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after the war, when was the war were finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: --right after the liberation, and you were so weak from hunger--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, after the war, after the liberation, that's the way when they registered me after all the papers what I have when the war was finished and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is 'they' registered you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The CIA. CIA. They ask you-- they talk-- they call you into an office. They ask you why you was in concentration camp. I told them very foolish things-- because I was born a Jew. That's the only reason. Why-- the reason-- tell him why I was in concentration camp. I told him, just because I am Jewish. That's the only reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the American CIA that is asking you this.

speaker: interviewee
text: American CIA. The American CIA, yeah. And then it was a little town, Ebensee. So I told you this. You went-- well, it was already easy. You could get food. It was a kitchen. You could eat food. You could-- it wasn't what it was-- you have to watch again because that's the way my brother died. He lived through the war. He start to eat right away whatever they gave you. The Americans gave you food in the cans. Cold food, when you start to eat this, this kills you. This was the reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They give you food, what you have over there, in the condensed cans. That's what they give you. So you start to eat, and then-- so you have to watch them eat. You have to-- you have to have a little bit-- use your brains what to do from so much-- from so many hunger. It's-- so you have to watch it. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] so it was a lot of religious Jews. So they form [? holiday. ?] In the evening, they used to pray. And I go out one Sunday, and I met the rabbi from my town. He used to go-- he-- he wasn't living as a-- as a Jew. He was living as a-- as a priest, something like this. But he lived through the war. And I met the rabbi from my little town. I have them on the picture over here. I met the rabbi. And then I used-- we went out and we met that German, the German who was in the army, officer, whatever he was-- that he took-- he took me to work in the garage. He took my friend to work in the kitchen. And then-- and then I went to-- then I went there. I don't know for how long, a month or two, I was working by them in the garage, sleeping with the army, everything to eat in there. I used to-- I used to work in the garage, for instance. This was in Germany-- Starnberg by-- by Munich, Starnberg, in Germany. No, no, this was in Ebensee, in Austria. But after being with them-- with the army two or three weeks, they transferred out our columns to Bayern, Germany. And they took me with them. And I was in Germany. There was Starnberg on Bordensee. This was beautiful, by the sea. And I was with them working in the garage. For instance, if-- I used to-- I used to drive us to the [? Becker. ?] I used to go to pick up gasoline. They have certain places where they have gasoline in that tanks. I used to pick up this and bring this there. When our-- when our-- when our-- when our Jeep got a flat, I used to go and fix the flat for them. Yeah, I was working there. I was very handy with this. And [LAUGHS] I used to-- I'm telling you, I'm lucky. For instance, they used to have those water pumps all over. So they-- they couldn't fix it, that water pump. And they-- and they called me in. I didn't know nothing, believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw them staying in that place. I said, and get out, get out, all of you, get out. I went in. I touched something. And the pump goes. [LAUGHS] And the water pump rolls. But luck-- a lot of things, I have an idea. But I wasn't specialized in those diesel engines like they have.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to work on gasoline engines, four cylinder, but I didn't-- I used to spoil them cars. If I spoil a car, nobody fix it. Nobody could fix it. I used to plug in the-- how do you call-- the exhaust pipe. You plug it in from outside with the [INAUDIBLE]. I plug it in outside so the engine is the same thing like you close your mouth, you can't talk. The engine can't breathe. And they couldn't start. And they didn't know what's going on. Yeah, I used to do a lot of things. I used to spoil them lathes. For instance, I was working on a lathe. And I got files there. And I chipped off a piece of file. And I throw it in into the gears-- 'vrrrrrr' --broke everything. Came the-- the foreman came. And he put down on the lathe a sign, 'Maschine kaput,' Maschine kaput, the machine is not working. That's all. I used to do a lot of things, what I-- anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your revenge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHTER] I used to do it, a lot of things. I do a lot of things I don't remember, you see. It's so many things, so many years. It's already 50 years. It's not a day or two. But you can forget, you see. Almost every night and day I'm dreaming. That's what-- that's what I think killed my wife because she was always-- whenever she walked up-- I felt she's scared. I woke her up. Regina, Regina. That's what came to her head. That's why she's-- she's sick now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you met her. Tell me how you-- what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina. Tell me how you met Regina.

speaker: interviewee
text: How I met Regina. I was walking on the street, like I told you, in Germany, just to look for revenge. And in this time, was right from the beginning. In 1945, I think, right from the beginning, they haven't got-- the police was just without guns going around, the German police, just-- just [INAUDIBLE] [? rubber ?] [? straps. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Batons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And out walking on the street, I see once about three boys and about three girls, about five, six-- they're going, they're talking Polish. So I-- and I see they're going. The German police-- a hat-- goes with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ask him, what's going on? Where are you going? So they told me, they was giving away meat on carts by a butcher. And they didn't want to stay in line. They went to the front. So the German policeman takes them on the police station.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I-- I go to that German policeman, what are you doing? I took away that strap. And excuse me for the expression, I kick him in the ass, and go. And I took him-- no, that's not everything. I took him by the collar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- Jewish, they have, like, a black market. And I brought him on that black market there. And there was also the same guy, Lieberman, and they want to kill him there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he kneels on his knees. And with hands like this, I'm never going to start with that Jew no more. And we let him go. Yeah, this was-- this time I met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- it was another-- another thing. That guy, what he told me-- what I told him, I'm going to live through that hell. And I got-- and I meet you. He came. My wife didn't know what he was in the camps. But he knows-- she knows him. He came with-- with him and another three guys and three girls, they came from another town to that place where I used to live, where I met her. So she didn't know that he was such a bad man in the camp. This time I met her. And I saw that she's a very, very nice girl, very, very easy. She didn't need nothing. She was happy with everything. That's what I-- I had a very good life with her for 40 years, over 40 years. I met her there. And I have the marriage license with me over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met her and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then  we used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, no, then I met her for a-- maybe for a-- I-- I met her in 1945. Or I met her-- I married her in 1948 or 1949. And then her father was in Russia during the war. A lot of people run away from Poland. The same thing-- they smuggled the border. They came to the border, [? Pilzno ?] or something, the Russian border. And they went over there. And he lived through the war in Russia. After the war, he came back. And he find out-- and he find out that she's alive. And he came back, and he married another woman over here, 20 years younger than him. She's still alive. And we met him. And she was with his father, in Stuttgart, before I met her. Father was very good to her. And I used to come to them. I have pictures to them-- with them. And then I married her. Before-- it was already-- I had already pictures, papers. I know my cousin is going to send papers for me to [? enter ?] United States. So I wanted-- I know I'm going to marry her. So I married her. And he sent me papers for her, too, and we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States with-- with $5. I didn't have nothing. And we got an apartment by a woman, Mrs. Goldman. And she asked me, you don't cook nothing? So I told her, we told her, no, we eat outside. We didn't have money to buy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, or a few days later, I walk on the street, I met a woman what we was together in Germany, from our-- from our-- from my friends. And she had already $50. She told me, you have money? I told her, I have no money. So she gave us $15 or $20 at this time. And I was already a millionaire.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] I had $40. You know what that means. Then she got-- I got a job. I got a job by Barton's Candy Store. They have a little machine shop on DeKalb Avenue in Brooklyn. And I start to work on Monday. And Friday was payday. Friday, I didn't have $0.15 in this time for the train to go to work. I walked from-- from-- from-- from Borough Park from Brooklyn to DeKalb Avenue-- three hours, something like this. When I got my first-- my first payday, I was already-- a mensch I was already. And then she starts to work. She was working in a factory where they make pillowcases on a sewing machine. And she was working. And we got-- we left that [? furnished ?] room. And I rented an apartment. And the-- and the life starts to go on. We were working good. I was working on two jobs but to make money, buy a television, furniture, a car, and everything. And I had a very good life with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did-- and you started a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1951, my daughter was born in the United States-- in the United States, [? Fifteenth ?] Avenue Doctor's Hospital in 1951, March the 12th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam, Miriam. And then later on, she went-- she finishes high school. She went to Brooklyn College. She met that-- her husband now, Lee Green, in Brooklyn College. And he went-- and she-- and then she finished college. She went at night for, I recall it, for two years. And then she finished. And he finished Brooklyn College. And he finished Brooklyn College, and they married. I make them a-- I have the picture from the wedding over here. I make them a wedding. Then he went for a specialty. She was-- oh, yeah. Oh, when she finished college, she got a job, but as a teacher. She teach math. At Lafayette High School in Brooklyn, she teaches math. And then she met him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they married. And after he went to Los Angeles for a specialty-- Los Angeles, City of Hope, hematology and oncology, blood disease and cancer-- when he finished this, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- I think they moved over here before, I don't remember exactly. I think they moved to Los-- they moved-- yeah, they moved over here. And they got an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah, this is-- and then they moved over here. And he got-- he was working. Where he was working? Oh, oh, no. While he was still in school, when he was finishing the school, two people came to the school-- if the school could recommend somebody in this-- in this-- in this hematology and oncology, they're looking for somebody, a good doctor. So they recommend him. And they took him in. And later, he became a partner. And now he has an office for himself in Cedars-Sinai Hospital. And she works in Los Angeles, Yeshiva University Med.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they got the two kids. One is-- one was two years, [? Leslie ?] had bar mitzvah. And the other one is going to be, I think, next year, something like this, yeah. And they bought a house. And they are very-- they are living a life. I mean, very-- he's a very, very nice man. His mother died-- she was 42 years-- died for cancer, I think, in Brooklyn, before the wedding. And his father married another time, a very nice woman. They live in Florida. And he has still in Brooklyn, in Long Island, a sister. She's going to have a baby today or tomorrow because they coming home tonight from the-- they went skiing someplace, Utah or someplace. So he's going to go. But they know now what's going to be a boy or a girl. They know before-- before the birth. So he's going to go to the briss. Then I see them two, three-- two times a week. And they call almost every night. I call them, they call me. The kids are very close to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever talk-- did you ever talk to your daughter about your experiences when she was growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom. She find out-- she find out-- she-- she went to-- to see She find out. I didn't tell her that things. I never tell her. What does she have to know? She find out. Now she-- and now I tell her all the things. Because I tell her, you see this and this. I told her everything in advance by Schindler because I was by Schindler. So I tell her, I'll tell her everything. She knows everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife, Regina, had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: She has-- this was the [? death camp ?]. She was-- she was lucky, too-- young when she lived through that hell. But she has the number over here on her hands, yeah. She was in Auschwitz. She was liberated in 1945, too, with-- with a few girlfriends-- what they all live in Israel. When I went to [? visit ?] Israel four years ago, I met them. They live in Beersheba. They was by me visiting over here, too from-- she has a few friends living in Israel. Yeah, I still have a cousin in Israel. His mother and my father, they was brother and sister. Yeah, so still over here. It's-- it's-- it's already so many years after the war. Even if they lived through the war, they're dying now because it's no-- it can be, you see-- you didn't have-- the youngster, the young-- the youngest-- the youngest in camp was-- that-- that my friend what [? Lebonier, ?] he was one of-- of-- of 20,000. The youngest from us was 15, 16 years, 17 years old in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the camps, it was terrible. [? Except ?] this-- you-- you lived like an animal because you lay down on that wooden bridges, we called them. You lay down with a-- with a human being. In the morning, you wake up, you touch him. What the hell? You're not moving. He's dead. He died. They were dying.

speaker: interviewee
text: The French people, they used to die terrible. Somehow they have-- they can't take that-- very, very soft people. The strongest was the Russians. They-- they die a lot because the Russians, whatever food they get, he was giving away for cigarettes. So they didn't eat. They smoke a lot, so they die out, too. But you had to-- oh, brother, they used to-- always used to hear at night-- waah, waah-- they're killing, they're hitting us, always. But it didn't bother you. You went-- you went out at night. At night, you see somebody lays-- lays down. And I would jump over him. And I went my way. No, no, no human-- you see people lying dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you jumped over him, and you went your way. What do you think kept you so strong? Where was the strength coming from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we was young and healthy. That's the strength came from. But after the war, I had my share of it. I had three bypass operations, sure, at Cedars-Sinai Hospital. I came to visit my daughter at 1 o'clock at night. I felt-- I wake up my daughter, Miriam. I said, I don't feel good somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not myself. So he called up right away his friend. And 1 o'clock at night, they took me-- they make a-- how do you call this? Sonogram or something like this, sonogram. And they-- and they saw it's-- four veins were collapsed. The blood didn't go through. But I feel good. I mean, it was 1948. 19-- what am I talking? 1988, 1988. Yeah, I had four bypass operations, sure. And you were telling me about your wife before. Is there anything else that you want to-- to let us know about her and your life together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well-- well, she-- well, she-- she-- when-- she was born with a spot on the lungs, with a spot on the lungs. So going to the United States, they made that x-rays and everything, and they saw that spot on the lungs. So they wanted-- they want to send her back for six months and make another picture in six months later. [? It ?] [? was ?] [? on ?] the same thing. If it's not worse, [? if it's ?] [? not ?] better, it means it's all right. But somehow-- somehow-- somehow I push her through. But when we came-- before we went on the boat in Bremenhaven in Germany, they separated us. She goes separate, and I went separate to sleep. I had with me $200, leaving Germany to the United States. So when I come up looking for her, well, she's-- she's sitting in a little bed and crying with gypsy women there. She cries, look where I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I find there somebody, what they told me, for money, you could get a separate room. So I went down. It was a guy who took care of the rooms, and then everything. And I ask him, how much is going to cost me for a separate room for myself and my wife? So he told me, $30, something like this. So he have $30, I got a separate room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they used-- used-- we used to be there two weeks. So I used to take the woman every day to peel potatoes, to work, to clean. She was crying, I-- I-- I don't feel like to go to work over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So again to him, how much it's going to cost me, she should stay home, she shouldn't go? $25. That's the way I spent the money that I came to the United States with. $5-- no, $10. Because on the boat, they gave her $5, and they gave me $5. So we had $10 we came to the United States. So, you know-- so it was very hard. But she was happy with everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to United States, my cousin was waiting for me on the boat. And they had prepared for me a furnished room. And he was in-- he was in chicken business. He was chicken-- chicken business. He sent me every week for Saturday a chicken. [LAUGHS] Very nice people. They're all not alive no more. Everything is gone. So that's the whole story. And now, the story is like this. Four-- four years-- four years-- four years ago, five years ago, I still was with her in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I have pictures with her. And then it starts beginning, it starts to-- first of all, I didn't know about this. How could I know that something, some sickness like this exists-- Alzheimer's disease?

speaker: interviewee
text: So she-- I see her, she starts-- she looks, and she looks at the clock. And she didn't know what time it is all of a sudden. So I told her, you know what it is. She told me, what time is it? So I told her. Then we are going home, she passed her-- she was going first. And I see she passed her house where she lives. So I tell her, you know, where are you going? She said, we're going home. I said, this is your house. Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then it starts like this. She-- she put-- first of all, she put away things what I never could find it. She didn't know, either, where she put away the things. Then I used to go, like, over here for-- to the gym. Like, over here, I used to go Family Fitness Center or JCC, over there with Jack Lalanne. I used to go and come home. She's not home. She walked out. I have to call police until they find her, walking someplace. She doesn't know. And that's what starts. And then a lot of problems with her-- I used to take her to the doctors over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they make-- and they take-- how do you call that hat that they check everything? And they told me, that is the problem. Her brains are affected. I was afraid it was a tumor or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I brought her over-- I came over here to Los Angeles. And they took her over here for a complete checkup. And that's what started. And I couldn't-- I couldn't take care no more in New York. I used to fall to sleep at night. And the landlady called me up, Nate, Regina is outside. She walked out at 1 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I know that the time is going to come that I have to give her away. This is her daughter. So it's hard to-- I don't want to have the responsibility on myself. She's going to tell me later, why you give her away? Why you do this? So I told her, find an apartment. I move to Los Angeles. It's going to be now in January three years where I'm over here. January 28 I came to-- to Los Angeles. So we moved over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over here, I have her in the house for a month, something like this. And I took a Polish woman. She should help me out with her. And I couldn't-- I couldn't manage with her. She used to cry all night, walk around. The Polish woman used to cook. She used to take a pot with hot water and throw it down.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's very jealous. She don't let me talk to nobody. For this, she has brains. Somebody talks to me, she like, shakes like this. I ask her, you know, what happened? I saw it. I saw it. What you saw it? She-- she's very jealous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she-- she didn't-- and now once-- once I went-- Yom Kippur, I went to shul. And I left her with a woman. No, I left her with the landlady.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the landlady's husband comes to-- to me to the shul. Nathan, come out. Regina's running away. She don't want to stay with us. So I have to come out from the shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I have to give her away. So I had her for two years in another place. And it was very dirty, [INAUDIBLE] a very religious place. And they-- they pack-- one on the other was sitting. Dirty, she always was smelling. And I paid so much money for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to come there. A Russian woman used to come to play piano for them. And that Russian woman told me, Nathan-- she saw I'm coming every day-- why you have to keep her over here in a such a schmutz around such dirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go over there. It's right there, two blocks away. It's a very Russian place, five-- five women. And it cost you the same money. She is treated like a king. And I come over there, I thought, I'm in my apartment. It's cleaner than my apartment. And I called up my daughter. And she came and saw it's clean. She has her separate room, beautiful, clean. The daughter left a deposit right away. And the next day, I moved her. And it's going to be now a year she's in this place, very, very clean. They cook there. They bake there. They take care. She-- she doesn't smell. She's clean. And that's all. She-- like this, she's healthy. Because I have a doctor from Century City, Dr. [? Trager. ?] He comes to her once a month, something like this. She's-- so she's taking pills, only anti-depression pills, Tegretol, to relax her a little bit, and takes pills for sleeping, something like this. And I go to her every day. She knows I'm there. Sometimes I take her out for a walk. So I tell her, Regina, when you're going to feel good, when you're going to feel cold, tell me. So she says, I'm cold already. Sometimes she knows, what-- sometimes she cries. I don't know what happened. I don't remember nothing. Most of the time she talks, she talks with-- I don't know what she's talking. I don't know, she talks, she talks. And she-- she-- you see, she knows that she's not home because she used to live there in one apartment for 23 or 25 years. When she wakes up, she looks up at the ceiling, she looks around. She sees that she's not in the place where she used to be. But it's terrible. I feel sorry for her. You live without a person for so many years, you have no problems. For the 40 years-- lived with her. So it's-- it's terrible. You feel alone. Whenever I came home, she used to be in the window, waiting for me. Now you come home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing. The-- the walls-- talk to the walls.
